title: The case against celebrity endorsements in local elections
summary: Argue whether celebrity endorsements help or harm democratic participation in local elections, and consider the structural distortions they create.
topic: The case against celebrity endorsements in local elections
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) examining whether celebrity endorsements in local elections — city council races, mayoral contests, school board votes — do more harm than good to democratic participation. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete example. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a local election is actually about is the kind of granular decision-making that…
Admittedly, celebrity endorsements have been shown to raise turnout in some demographic…
Having dominated the national conversation for weeks, a single celebrity tweet can…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bầu cử địa phương
sự chứng thực / ủng hộ của người nổi tiếng
sự tham gia dân chủ
độ nhận diện tên tuổi
làm méo mó diễn ngôn / cuộc tranh luận
sự mất cân bằng mang tính cấu trúc
huy động cử tri
sự tham gia vào các vấn đề công dân
nội dung chính sách thực chất
lu mờ / làm mờ nhạt
mối quan hệ đơn chiều (người hâm mộ cảm thấy gần gũi với người nổi tiếng)
tràn ngập không gian thông tin
hành vi bỏ phiếu thực tế
quá trình thẩm định ứng viên
một nền dân chủ hoạt động tốt
nhầm lẫn sự nổi tiếng với năng lực
quy định phân vùng (quy hoạch đô thị)
lợi thế của người đương nhiệm
khuếch đại một tín hiệu
đặt nội dung thực chất xuống dưới sự hào nhoáng

sampleText:
Local elections are, almost by design, exercises in granular democracy. Voters are asked to weigh candidates on their positions on bus routes, school meal budgets, and planning applications — the unglamorous machinery of daily civic life. What a celebrity endorsement introduces into this environment is not information but noise: a signal calibrated for national audiences that drowns out the quieter, more relevant voices of ward councillors and community organisers.

The distorting effect is well-documented, even if its precise scale is contested. When a major recording artist endorsed a candidate in the 2018 Tennessee Senate race, the campaign's donation page reportedly crashed within hours and new voter registrations spiked — but this was a statewide contest. Scaled down to a city council seat, the same dynamic concentrates power around candidates who can attract famous supporters rather than those who understand how a drainage report works. Candidates are then filtered not by local knowledge but by the ability to perform well in spaces where celebrities feel comfortable engaging.

Admittedly, voter turnout in local elections is genuinely low, and anything that pulls people toward the ballot box has some democratic value. A celebrity post that reaches two million followers may register a race that would otherwise pass unnoticed. This is not a trivial point. A city council seat affects thousands of people; having it decided by forty thousand voters is arguably worse than having it decided by eighty thousand who were partly motivated by a tweet.

Even so, what is being mobilised matters as much as how many. Voters drawn in by parasocial loyalty to a celebrity rather than by engagement with policy substance are unlikely to develop the habit of attending council meetings, reading planning applications, or holding elected officials accountable between elections. Captured in a single endorsement cycle, they are gone by the next one. What a well-functioning democracy requires at the local level is not a spike in registration but a sustained culture of informed participation — and celebrity endorsements, precisely because they subordinate substance to spectacle, tend to work against that culture.

Granted, the answer is not to silence celebrities or to restrict political speech. It is, rather, to recognise that the question 'who are you voting for?' and the question 'why does this election matter?' are different questions, and that a system calibrated around the first at the expense of the second produces worse local government, not better.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to foreground a key opposition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a celebrity endorsement introduces into this environment is not information but noise' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để tạo sự đối lập mạnh mẽ. Thay vì viết 'A celebrity endorsement introduces noise, not information', câu chẻ đôi đặt sự đối lập ở cuối câu — vị trí nhấn mạnh nhất — và đặt 'celebrity endorsement' vào vị trí chủ ngữ logic rõ ràng hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a celebrity endorsement introduces into this environment is not information but noise' uses a what-cleft to foreground a sharp opposition. Instead of 'A celebrity endorsement introduces noise, not information', the cleft places the contrast at the end — the most emphatic position — and clarifies the logical subject of the argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Participle clause to compress a conditional observation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Captured in a single endorsement cycle, they are gone by the next one' dùng mệnh đề phân từ bị động ('Captured in…') để nén một điều kiện ngầm: 'Because they are captured only in a single endorsement cycle'. Cấu trúc này tạo ra nhịp điệu ngắn gọn và có tính phán xét, phù hợp với văn phong nghị luận.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Captured in a single endorsement cycle, they are gone by the next one' uses a passive participle clause to compress an implicit conditional: 'Because they are captured only in a single endorsement cycle'. This structure creates a terse, judgemental rhythm suited to argumentative prose.

sampleTranslationVi:
Các cuộc bầu cử địa phương, gần như theo thiết kế, là những bài tập trong nền dân chủ chi tiết. Cử tri được yêu cầu cân nhắc các ứng viên dựa trên quan điểm của họ về lộ trình xe buýt, ngân sách bữa ăn học đường và các đơn xin cấp phép xây dựng — bộ máy không hào nhoáng của cuộc sống công dân hàng ngày. Điều mà sự ủng hộ của người nổi tiếng mang vào môi trường này không phải là thông tin mà là tiếng ồn: một tín hiệu được hiệu chỉnh cho khán giả toàn quốc, làm chìm đi những tiếng nói nhỏ hơn nhưng phù hợp hơn của các ủy viên hội đồng phường và các nhà tổ chức cộng đồng.

Hiệu ứng méo mó đã được ghi chép đầy đủ, dù quy mô chính xác của nó vẫn còn tranh cãi. Khi một nghệ sĩ thu âm lớn ủng hộ một ứng viên trong cuộc đua Thượng viện Tennessee năm 2018, trang quyên góp của chiến dịch được cho là đã bị sập trong vài giờ và số lượng đăng ký cử tri mới tăng vọt — nhưng đây là một cuộc tranh cử cấp bang. Thu nhỏ xuống một ghế hội đồng thành phố, động lực tương tự sẽ tập trung quyền lực xung quanh các ứng viên có thể thu hút những người ủng hộ nổi tiếng hơn là những người hiểu cách một báo cáo thoát nước hoạt động. Các ứng viên sau đó được lọc không phải bởi kiến thức địa phương mà bởi khả năng thể hiện tốt trong các không gian mà người nổi tiếng cảm thấy thoải mái khi tham gia.

Thừa nhận rằng, tỷ lệ cử tri đi bỏ phiếu trong các cuộc bầu cử địa phương thực sự rất thấp, và bất cứ điều gì kéo mọi người đến hòm phiếu đều có một số giá trị dân chủ. Một bài đăng của người nổi tiếng tiếp cận hai triệu người theo dõi có thể ghi lại một cuộc đua mà nếu không sẽ trôi qua mà không ai chú ý. Đây không phải là một điểm tầm thường. Một ghế hội đồng thành phố ảnh hưởng đến hàng nghìn người; việc nó được quyết định bởi bốn mươi nghìn cử tri có thể tệ hơn so với tám mươi nghìn người, một phần có động lực từ một tweet.

Dẫu vậy, những gì đang được huy động quan trọng không kém số lượng. Cử tri bị thu hút bởi lòng trung thành đơn chiều với một người nổi tiếng hơn là bởi sự tham gia vào nội dung chính sách thực chất ít có khả năng phát triển thói quen tham dự các cuộc họp hội đồng, đọc các đơn xin cấp phép xây dựng hoặc yêu cầu các quan chức được bầu chịu trách nhiệm giữa các cuộc bầu cử. Bị cuốn vào một chu kỳ chứng thực duy nhất, họ sẽ biến mất vào chu kỳ tiếp theo. Điều mà một nền dân chủ hoạt động tốt đòi hỏi ở cấp địa phương không phải là sự tăng đột biến trong đăng ký mà là một văn hóa tham gia có hiểu biết bền vững — và các chứng thực của người nổi tiếng, chính xác vì chúng đặt nội dung thực chất xuống dưới sự hào nhoáng, có xu hướng đi ngược lại văn hóa đó.

Thừa nhận rằng, câu trả lời không phải là im lặng người nổi tiếng hoặc hạn chế ngôn luận chính trị. Thay vào đó, là nhận ra rằng câu hỏi 'bạn đang bỏ phiếu cho ai?' và câu hỏi 'tại sao cuộc bầu cử này quan trọng?' là những câu hỏi khác nhau, và một hệ thống được hiệu chỉnh xung quanh câu hỏi đầu tiên với cái giá của câu hỏi thứ hai tạo ra chính quyền địa phương tệ hơn, không phải tốt hơn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a 'what' cleft to highlight a contrast?
options: What a celebrity endorsement introduces into this environment is not information but noise.
A celebrity endorsement introduces what is not information but noise into this environment.
Into this environment what celebrity endorsements introduce is noise not information.
Not information but noise is what a celebrity endorsement into this environment introduces.
mcQuestions.explanation: The standard what-cleft structure is 'What [subject + verb] is [complement]', placing the contrast at the end for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that begins 'What … is …' with the contrast after 'is'.

options: A formal qualification awarded to celebrities by universities
A public statement by a famous person expressing support for a candidate or product
A type of advertisement that celebrities are legally required to approve
A contract between a celebrity and a political party
mcQuestions.explanation: An endorsement is a public declaration of support; a celebrity endorsement means a famous person lends their name and following to a candidate or cause.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'endorse' as 'to publicly back' — who is doing the backing here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive construction to background the agent?
options: Researchers have well-documented the distorting effect of celebrity endorsements.
The distorting effect is well-documented, even if its precise scale is contested.
The distorting effect documents well the celebrity endorsements' influence.
Celebrity endorsements document the distorting effect in a well-known way.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The distorting effect is well-documented' uses passive voice to foreground what is known without specifying who documented it — appropriate for general academic claims.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes the effect itself the grammatical subject, using 'is [past participle]'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to subordinate substance to spectacle' mean?
options: To prioritise policy content over entertaining performance
To place real content in a lower position of importance than flashy performance
To combine serious arguments with entertaining examples
To replace complicated policies with simpler ones
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To subordinate X to Y' means to place X in a lower or less important position than Y. Here, 'substance' (real content) is being ranked below 'spectacle' (entertaining show).
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'subordinate' do to something — elevate it or place it beneath something else?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession sentence is correctly formed?
options: Admittedly, voter turnout in local elections is genuinely low, and anything that pulls people toward the ballot box has some democratic value.
Admittedly voter turnout is genuinely low in local elections but anything pulling people ballot box has democratic value.
Voter turnout admittedly in local elections genuinely low is and anything democratic value has.
Admittedly, voter turnout local elections genuinely low are and democratic value anything has.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' is followed by a grammatically complete clause, then a coordinated clause with 'and' — this is the standard concession structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'Admittedly,' followed by a full independent clause.

options: A friendship between two people in the same profession
A one-sided emotional connection a fan feels toward a celebrity who does not know them
A legal partnership between a celebrity and their management team
A social media network exclusively for famous people
mcQuestions.explanation: A parasocial relationship is an imagined intimacy felt by an audience member toward a public figure — the celebrity is unaware of the individual fan.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Para-' means alongside or beside — here it signals a relationship that resembles but is not truly social.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause as an opening modifier?
options: They are gone by the next endorsement cycle, having been captured once.
Captured in a single endorsement cycle, they are gone by the next one.
A single endorsement cycle captures them, and they are gone by the next.
Being gone by the next cycle, they were captured in a single endorsement.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Captured in a single endorsement cycle' is a passive participle clause that opens the sentence, compressing an implied causal or conditional relationship.
mcQuestions.hint: The participle clause should come first, before the main clause subject.

options: Getting married in a registry office
Active participation in the political and social life of a community
Attending concerts and cultural events in a city
Paying municipal taxes on time
mcQuestions.explanation: Civic engagement refers to actions — voting, attending meetings, volunteering — through which citizens participate in the governance and life of their community.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Civic' relates to citizenship and community — what kind of activity connects citizens to their local government?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to conflate popularity with competence' mean?
options: To assume that well-known people must be skilled at their job
To promote both popular and competent candidates equally
To distinguish carefully between fame and ability
To measure a candidate's competence by how many votes they receive
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To conflate' means to wrongly merge two separate things; here, treating a candidate as skilled simply because they are well-known.
mcQuestions.hint: Conflate means to mix up or incorrectly treat as the same — what two things are being merged here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from a concession back to the main argument?
options: Similarly, voter turnout is also a problem in national elections.
Even so, what is being mobilised matters as much as how many.
For example, a celebrity can post about a local candidate.
Therefore, we should encourage more celebrities to endorse candidates.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the force of the concession and then reasserts the writer's position — the standard concession-rebuttal pivot.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option signals 'despite that point, my argument still holds'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'name recognition' mean in an electoral context?
options: The legal requirement for voters to present identification at polling stations
Voters knowing a candidate's name without necessarily knowing their policies
A candidate's ability to remember the names of constituents
The process by which party names are registered with electoral authorities
mcQuestions.explanation: Name recognition means voters are familiar with a candidate's name — which can translate into votes even in the absence of policy knowledge.
mcQuestions.hint: Recognition here is about awareness — knowing the name exists, not knowing what it stands for.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal register?
options: It's kind of weird that celebs can basically decide local elections just by tweeting about them.
The capacity of celebrity endorsements to distort candidate selection in local contests raises substantive democratic concerns.
Famous people tweeting about elections is obviously a big problem for democracy.
Celebs have always loved getting involved in politics and honestly it's a bit much.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The capacity of … to distort … raises substantive democratic concerns' uses precise, abstract nouns and formal collocations appropriate to academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid contractions, hedges like 'kind of', and informal intensifiers like 'obviously' or 'honestly'.

options: A physical booklet containing a party's policy documents
The real, concrete content of a candidate's policy positions and proposals
The chemical composition of a drug approved under government policy
A formal agreement between two political parties
mcQuestions.explanation: Policy substance refers to the actual content and detail of what a candidate proposes to do — as opposed to their image, personality, or celebrity connections.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Substance' contrasts with 'surface' or 'style' — what is the deeper content of a policy?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses inversion for emphasis?
options: Rarely does a celebrity endorsement translate directly into meaningful civic engagement.
A celebrity endorsement rarely does translate directly into meaningful civic engagement.
Rarely a celebrity endorsement translates directly into meaningful civic engagement.
A celebrity endorsement does rarely translate directly into meaningful civic engagement.
mcQuestions.explanation: After a negative or restrictive adverb like 'Rarely', the auxiliary verb ('does') moves before the subject ('a celebrity endorsement') — this is standard inversion for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'Rarely', does the auxiliary come before or after the subject?

options: The benefits of retiring from public office
The natural advantage a current officeholder has in an election, due to visibility and experience
The financial advantage that wealthy candidates enjoy
The advantage given to candidates who run in their home district
mcQuestions.explanation: Incumbents (current officeholders) benefit from name recognition, media access, and a record to point to — advantages that challengers lack.
mcQuestions.hint: An 'incumbent' is someone already holding a position — what advantage does holding office give at election time?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best paraphrases 'a system calibrated around the first at the expense of the second'?
options: A system that balances two questions equally
A system designed to answer one question while sacrificing the other
A system that improves over time through calibration
A system that rewards expensive candidates over affordable ones
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Calibrated around X at the expense of Y' means the system is tuned or optimised for X, and as a result Y suffers — one is gained at the cost of the other.
mcQuestions.hint: 'At the expense of' signals a trade-off — gaining one thing costs another.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever been influenced by the opinion of a celebrity you admire when making a decision that had nothing to do with their area of expertise? On reflection, do you think their influence was helpful or misplaced — and does your answer change when the decision involves voting rather than, say, choosing a product?
